howItWorks.intro: This marine holding tank calculator uses established formulas to provide accurate results.

rules.name: Tank Size
rules.explanation: Per-person daily usage with 25% safety margin.

howItWorks.outro: Results are estimates based on standard formulas. Verify with current local data for your specific situation.

faq.question: How big should a holding tank be?
faq.answer: Plan 2 gallons per person per day, then add 25% buffer.

faq.question: How often to pump out?
faq.answer: Depends on tank size and crew. Most cruisers pump every 3-5 days.

faq.question: Is the Marine Holding Tank Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard boating and marine formulas trusted by boaters and sailors. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What people aboard should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate people aboard value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

sections.title: What Is the Marine Holding Tank Calculator?
sections.body: The Marine Holding Tank Calculator is a free online tool designed for boaters and sailors who need quick, accurate calculations in the boating and marine space. By entering your people aboard, days between pump-outs, you get instant results including minimum tank size, recommended size. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting minimum tank size right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In boating and marine, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by boaters and sailors worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to navigate safely with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your people aboard and need to find the right minimum tank size. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your People Aboard
howToSteps.description: Type or select your people aboard in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Days Between Pump-Outs
howToSteps.description: Type or select your days between pump-outs in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your minimum tank size, recommended size. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your people aboard before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both minimum tank size and recommended size — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to navigate safely.
If you're unsure about your days between pump-outs, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
